sections.text: The ballad begins with twenty-seven lines of poetic images of sensual attraction and the strong feelings associated with particular social relationships (lines 1-16.1), concluded by an authorial aside (line 17), shifting attention to the start of narrative.

In agricultural fields outside a village, women leave two babies lying together as they work nearby (lines 18-20.1). Uncovering the male and female infants, an elder woman finds them in an intimate, adult embrace (lines 21-6).
The woman calls the spirits for help and tells the others what she saw (lines 27-47).
The eponymous girl and the boy are identified by name and praise epithets (lines 48-54.1).

They grow to marriageable age and one day, as they are returning home from their tasks outside the village, they meet (lines 55-62). The young man immediately launches into a poetic seduction and a proposal to elope (lines 62.1-72.2). [She agrees]
After ten months or so of marriage, while making love, the young man remarks upon the absence of beads around Kaiyula's waist (lines 73-97).

The young woman begins to cry, the man to apologize, and finally, he promises to go on a journey of trade to bring her some waist beads (lines 98-102.1). She accompanies him to Lake Victoria and sees him off on his journey (lines 103-110.4).
The bard chants his own self-praise (lines 111-127) and then returns to the frame of the story (lines 134-35.1)
On her way back to the house, Kaiyula meets a neighbor, who asks about her situation and is very complimentary about her appearance; Kaiyula rejects the approach (lines 135.2-146).
When she returns home, she sees the neighbor ask the children of her household to send her an amorous message (lines 147-160).

Captivated by the man's words, the children neglect their tasks; Kaiyula criticizes them and a dialogue ensues in which the children express their belief that the man is a former lover of Kaiyula's; she orders them inside (161-183).
The neighbor approaches the house and begins his verbal seduction with sensual imagery, praise, and declarations of his loving desire, but Kaiyula rejects him (lines 184-200.1).
Importuned yet again, Kaiyula raises the objection of her absent husband and the magical harm her affair could cause him (lines 201-212.2).
The knowing neighbor tells her how to deflect this kind of danger with herbs and ritual acts (lines 213-232).
Kaiyula raises a further objection about her husband, but the neighbor answers it (lines 233-238.1).
In a final attempt to dissuade him, Kaiyula indicates she is menstruating, prompting a response that confirms to her the trueness of his loving desire (lines 239-247.1).

Kaiyula then consults her in-laws, under whose protection and supervision she lives, about the potential affair. Her sister-in-law sees no problem in it and instructs the young woman in rituals to protect her husband's possessions -- his clan land, his trading goods, and the attractiveness of her body -- from possible harm (lines 248-271).
She does as she was told (lines 272-280.1).

She then consults her husband's nephew, who in figurative language differentiates institutional and non-institutional forms of love and declares that latter harmless if handled correctly; Kaiyula agrees (lines 281-290).
Kaiyula remarks to the neighbor about the presence of her mother-in-law, but to him she poses no problem. His flattering words bend her will to his purpose (lines 291-317).
The mother-in-law agrees to let the man inside the house (lines 319-327.1).
Their elaborate pillow talk is playful argument, praise, encouragement, and ecstatic exclamation (lines 351-386).

Kaiyula's verbal expression, which had never before been so free, sparks anger in the mother-in-law. She now worries about the potential magical effect of the adultery and threatens the young woman (lines 387-395).
Kaiyula answers in a way that demonstrates a rhetorical power commensurate with that of the seductive neighbor (lines 396-406.1).

In a coda, the bard introduces two characters from other speech genres to comment on the ballad narrative\: a folktale bird that sees but says it will not tell, and a Biblical or Koranic angel that failed to maintain the sanctity of marriage (lines 409-413).
